title: Major Depression and Antidepressants
subtitle: The nurse cares for a depressed patient — assessing suicide risk (highest as energy returns), spending time without false cheer, and teaching that antidepressants take weeks and have key safety rules.

patient.name: Major Depression
patient.admitDate: The nurse supports a patient with major depressive disorder.

nursesNote: The nurse cares for a patient with major depressive disorder, a treatable illness marked by persistent low mood and loss of interest or pleasure (anhedonia) along with changes in sleep and appetite, fatigue, feelings of worthlessness or guilt, poor concentration, and possible thoughts of death or suicide. The priority is safety: the nurse asks directly about suicidal thoughts and a plan, knowing that suicide risk is highest early in treatment as energy begins to return, because the patient may still feel hopeless but now has the energy to act. The nurse spends unhurried time with the patient, uses a calm and accepting manner, avoids false cheerfulness or telling the patient to simply 'snap out of it,' offers simple choices, and gently encourages small, achievable activities to rebuild a sense of success. Antidepressants such as SSRIs are first-line and take about two to four weeks or longer for full effect, so the patient is taught not to stop early and not to discontinue abruptly. The nurse watches for serotonin syndrome (agitation, high fever, tremor, sweating, and rapid heart rate), especially when serotonergic drugs are combined, and teaches patients on MAOIs to avoid tyramine-rich foods (aged cheese, cured or smoked meats, and certain wines) to prevent a hypertensive crisis. The nurse supports medication adherence, therapy, and follow-up.
providerOrders: Assess suicide risk directly and maintain a safe environment
Increase observation as energy returns early in treatment
Spend time with the patient and avoid false reassurance
Teach that antidepressants take weeks and should not be stopped abruptly
Teach SSRI and MAOI safety, including tyramine avoidance

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about major depression?

opts.text: Anhedonia is loss of interest or pleasure
Sleep and appetite changes are common
Suicide risk should always be assessed
Telling the patient to 'cheer up' is therapeutic
Risk can rise as energy returns in treatment
Depression is a treatable illness
SSRIs take about 2-4 weeks for full effect
Antidepressants can be stopped abruptly safely
MAOIs require avoiding tyramine-rich foods
Serotonin syndrome includes fever and tremor
Spending unhurried time helps the patient
Small, achievable activities build success

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order admitting a severely depressed patient

opts.text: Assess suicide risk and ensure safety
Establish trust with calm presence
Meet basic needs: sleep, food, hygiene
Start medication and teach the time to effect
Encourage gradual activity and follow-up
Sitting with the patient without rushing
Saying 'You have so much to be happy about'
Offering simple, limited choices
Telling the patient to just think positive
Directly asking about suicidal thoughts
Planning a long list of tasks for today

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: A withdrawn patient suddenly seems calm, brighter, and is giving away belongings
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Recognize this as a possible warning sign
Ask directly about suicidal thoughts and a plan
Increase observation and do not leave alone
Notify the provider and team
Document the findings and interventions
Increasing observation as energy returns
Telling the patient SSRIs work the same day
Teaching MAOI patients to avoid aged cheese
Stopping the antidepressant once feeling better
Reporting agitation, fever, and tremor on serotonergic meds
Using false reassurance to lift the mood
Encouraging small, achievable daily activities
Avoiding any talk of suicide to not upset them
